mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows you to manage security and compliance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for platform engineering teams looking to enforce governance and automate policy management. Key trade-offs include the need for understanding policy definitions and potential performance impacts on cluster operations.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install Kyverno easily.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace, creating the namespace if it doesn't exist.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Creates the namespace for Kyverno.
> Installs Kyverno using the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify their status.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate how Kyverno can enforce rules.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> Creates a policy that requires the 'app' label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy is enforced
steps.description:
This command checks if the policy is working by attempting to create a Pod without the required label.

```bash

kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx --namespace=default

```

> Attempt to create a Pod without the required label to see if the policy is enforced.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno from the cluster. Be aware that this will delete all policies and associated resources managed by Kyverno.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls the Kyverno release.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version while maintaining your existing configurations.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing policies to a file.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Kyverno pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'validation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when a resource does not comply with the defined policies.

**Fix:**
Check the policy definitions and ensure that the resource meets the required criteria.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen due to misconfiguration or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' for details.

troubleshooting.title: Policies not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Policies may not be applied correctly or are misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Verify the policy status with 'kubectl get clusterpolicies' and check for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to create resources due to policy violations
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The resource being created does not comply with existing policies.

**Fix:**
Review the policy requirements and adjust the resource definition accordingly.

versionNotes.changes: Improved support for validating custom resource definitions (CRDs) and added new policy templates.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.

  name: require-labels

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and have kubectl and Helm installed.
